file.content:
# Lactate threshold modelling - simulation-based inference test case

This experimental repository defines and simulates from a mechanistic model of lactate accumulation under dynamic excercise loads. It will then build and validates an amortised Bayesian simulation-based inference engine, and applies it to empirical data with continuous heart rate monitoring and intermittent lactate testing, to infer a posterior over the lactate threshold and lactate threshold heart rate.

This is a simple test case of using agentic AI to perform amortised simulation-based Bayesian inference on a user-provided model and target dataset. This will use the agentic AI resources in https://github.com/idem-lab/bayesflow-sbi-agent-kit.

The main branch will contain the code and information prior to engaging agentic AI to support SBI. Worked agentic SBI analyses, with different versions of the above agent kit will be stored on different branchs.   

Note that the author is not an expert in exercise physiology, and the use case is a toy example, so the assumptions made in constructing this model should not be considered as remotely authoritative. Agentic AI is also being used to rapidly implement the initial code for this toy example and write documentation, so errors may well occur there too.

Below we define the model and data considered in this repo.

## Model

We define simple two-compartment model of lactate levels in muscle and blood, with a simple form of negative density dependence on lactate levels, under dynamic exercise load (external forcing). This model is inspired by the model of [Zouloumian & Freund (1981)](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428866), though that model focuses on lactate kinetics after the cessation of exercise, rather than under dynamic forcing of lactate.

### Assumptions:

1. Lactate is generated in working muscle, with the rate of lactate production increasing with the rate of work.

2. Lactate is transported between muscle and blood, with the rate of transport depending on the concentrations in the two compartments

3. Lactate is cleared from the blood, with a rate that increases with the blood lactate concentration, up to a maximum value.

### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol.L^-1) in the muscle (C_m) and blood (C_b) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

#### Lactate generation in muscle

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol.L^-1.min^-1) as \lambda, which we can model as linear in heart rate h(t) at time t, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

  \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)

where \alpha and \beta are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol.min^-1, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' P_m:

  \eta_m = P_m (C_m - C_b)

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate \eta_{threshold}), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability P_b, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value C_{min}; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

  \eta_b = \eta_{threshold} tanh(P_b (C_b - C_{min}) / \eta_{threshold})

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \eta_m / V_m
  dC_b/dt = \eta_m / V_b - eta_b / V_b

#### Reparameterisation

This system has 8 unknown parameters: V_m, V_b, P_m, P_b, C_{min}, \eta_{threshold}, \alpha, \beta. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the. uscle volume V_m.

We define rho as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:
  \rho = V_b / V_m

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:
  a_m = P_m / V_b
  a_b = P_b / V_b
  r_b = \eta_{threshold} / V_b

since:
  P_m / V_m = (P_m / V_b) (V_b / V_m) = \rho a_m 

we have the reparameterised model:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \rho a_m (C_m - C_b)
  dC_b/dt = a_m (C_m - C_b) - r_b tanh(a_b (C_b - C_{min}) / r_b)

with one fewer parameter:
 \rho > 0
 a_m > 0
 a_b > 0
 r_b > 0
 C_{min} > 0
 \alpha
 \beta > 0

where beta must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

### Sampling model

The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running. The protocol has participants warm up, stand stationary for 3 minutes, and then perform a continuous series of 3 minute efforts with increasing exercise intensity. Two relevant types of data are collected for each of these 3 minute periods (stationary and efforts)
Two types of data are available for each period, for each participant:
 - average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol.L^-1) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.

Since the amount of work is kept roughly constant within each period, we convert the average heart rate measuements into a piecewise constant continuous function on time: h(t). Given the reparameterised process equations and parameters listed above, we model a continuous-time function of blood lactate concentration as:

  \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)
  C_b(t) = \int_0^t dC_b/dt dt

We define a Gaussian sampling model for each blood lactate concentration measurement B_i using this modelled function, where t(i) denotes the time corresponding to blood lactate observation i - the end of each 3 minute period - and an observation standard deviation parameter \sigma:

  B_i ~ N(C_b(t(i)), \sigma^2)

### Priors

We should be able to define an informative prior on sigma from literature on the measurement error of lactate tests. For all other parameters we will have to define reasonable priors by prior predictive checking. As a heuristic, blood lactate in a sports setting (ie. not in a medical emergency) has been observed as high as 32 mmol.L^-1 [(Osnes & Hermansen, 1972)](https://doi.org/10.1152/jappl.1972.32.1.59).

## Data

We will infer the model parameters for each participant in the cycling and running records of a large dataset of graded incremental exercise tests, which give these average heart rates and blood lactate measurements: [Bernard et al. (2024)](https://zenodo.org/records/10841412).

input.new_string:
We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

#### Lactate generation in muscle

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹) as $\lambda$, which we can model as linear in heart rate $h(t)$ at time $t$, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:

$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}}\,\tanh\\!\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \eta_m / V_m
  dC_b/dt = \eta_m / V_b - eta_b / V_b

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Lactate threshold modelling - simulation-based inference test case

This experimental repository defines and simulates from a mechanistic model of lactate accumulation under dynamic excercise loads. It will then build and validates an amortised Bayesian simulation-based inference engine, and applies it to empirical data with continuous heart rate monitoring and intermittent lactate testing, to infer a posterior over the lactate threshold and lactate threshold heart rate.

This is a simple test case of using agentic AI to perform amortised simulation-based Bayesian inference on a user-provided model and target dataset. This will use the agentic AI resources in https://github.com/idem-lab/bayesflow-sbi-agent-kit.

The main branch will contain the code and information prior to engaging agentic AI to support SBI. Worked agentic SBI analyses, with different versions of the above agent kit will be stored on different branchs.   

Note that the author is not an expert in exercise physiology, and the use case is a toy example, so the assumptions made in constructing this model should not be considered as remotely authoritative. Agentic AI is also being used to rapidly implement the initial code for this toy example and write documentation, so errors may well occur there too.

Below we define the model and data considered in this repo.

## Model

We define simple two-compartment model of lactate levels in muscle and blood, with a simple form of negative density dependence on lactate levels, under dynamic exercise load (external forcing). This model is inspired by the model of [Zouloumian & Freund (1981)](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428866), though that model focuses on lactate kinetics after the cessation of exercise, rather than under dynamic forcing of lactate.

### Assumptions:

1. Lactate is generated in working muscle, with the rate of lactate production increasing with the rate of work.

2. Lactate is transported between muscle and blood, with the rate of transport depending on the concentrations in the two compartments

3. Lactate is cleared from the blood, with a rate that increases with the blood lactate concentration, up to a maximum value.

### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol.L^-1) in the muscle (C_m) and blood (C_b) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

#### Lactate generation in muscle

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol.L^-1.min^-1) as \lambda, which we can model as linear in heart rate h(t) at time t, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

  \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)

where \alpha and \beta are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol.min^-1, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' P_m:

  \eta_m = P_m (C_m - C_b)

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate \eta_{threshold}), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability P_b, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value C_{min}; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

  \eta_b = \eta_{threshold} tanh(P_b (C_b - C_{min}) / \eta_{threshold})

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \eta_m / V_m
  dC_b/dt = \eta_m / V_b - eta_b / V_b

#### Reparameterisation

This system has 8 unknown parameters: V_m, V_b, P_m, P_b, C_{min}, \eta_{threshold}, \alpha, \beta. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the. uscle volume V_m.

We define rho as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:
  \rho = V_b / V_m

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:
  a_m = P_m / V_b
  a_b = P_b / V_b
  r_b = \eta_{threshold} / V_b

since:
  P_m / V_m = (P_m / V_b) (V_b / V_m) = \rho a_m 

we have the reparameterised model:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \rho a_m (C_m - C_b)
  dC_b/dt = a_m (C_m - C_b) - r_b tanh(a_b (C_b - C_{min}) / r_b)

with one fewer parameter:
 \rho > 0
 a_m > 0
 a_b > 0
 r_b > 0
 C_{min} > 0
 \alpha
 \beta > 0

where beta must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

### Sampling model

The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running. The protocol has participants warm up, stand stationary for 3 minutes, and then perform a continuous series of 3 minute efforts with increasing exercise intensity. Two relevant types of data are collected for each of these 3 minute periods (stationary and efforts)
Two types of data are available for each period, for each participant:
 - average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol.L^-1) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.

Since the amount of work is kept roughly constant within each period, we convert the average heart rate measuements into a piecewise constant continuous function on time: h(t). Given the reparameterised process equations and parameters listed above, we model a continuous-time function of blood lactate concentration as:

  \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)
  C_b(t) = \int_0^t dC_b/dt dt

We define a Gaussian sampling model for each blood lactate concentration measurement B_i using this modelled function, where t(i) denotes the time corresponding to blood lactate observation i - the end of each 3 minute period - and an observation standard deviation parameter \sigma:

  B_i ~ N(C_b(t(i)), \sigma^2)

### Priors

We should be able to define an informative prior on sigma from literature on the measurement error of lactate tests. For all other parameters we will have to define reasonable priors by prior predictive checking. As a heuristic, blood lactate in a sports setting (ie. not in a medical emergency) has been observed as high as 32 mmol.L^-1 [(Osnes & Hermansen, 1972)](https://doi.org/10.1152/jappl.1972.32.1.59).

## Data

We will infer the model parameters for each participant in the cycling and running records of a large dataset of graded incremental exercise tests, which give these average heart rates and blood lactate measurements: [Bernard et al. (2024)](https://zenodo.org/records/10841412).

# Lactate threshold modelling - simulation-based inference test case

This experimental repository defines and simulates from a mechanistic model of lactate accumulation under dynamic excercise loads. It will then build and validates an amortised Bayesian simulation-based inference engine, and applies it to empirical data with continuous heart rate monitoring and intermittent lactate testing, to infer a posterior over the lactate threshold and lactate threshold heart rate.

This is a simple test case of using agentic AI to perform amortised simulation-based Bayesian inference on a user-provided model and target dataset. This will use the agentic AI resources in https://github.com/idem-lab/bayesflow-sbi-agent-kit.

The main branch will contain the code and information prior to engaging agentic AI to support SBI. Worked agentic SBI analyses, with different versions of the above agent kit will be stored on different branchs.   

Note that the author is not an expert in exercise physiology, and the use case is a toy example, so the assumptions made in constructing this model should not be considered as remotely authoritative. Agentic AI is also being used to rapidly implement the initial code for this toy example and write documentation, so errors may well occur there too.

Below we define the model and data considered in this repo.

## Model

We define simple two-compartment model of lactate levels in muscle and blood, with a simple form of negative density dependence on lactate levels, under dynamic exercise load (external forcing). This model is inspired by the model of [Zouloumian & Freund (1981)](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428866), though that model focuses on lactate kinetics after the cessation of exercise, rather than under dynamic forcing of lactate.

### Assumptions:

1. Lactate is generated in working muscle, with the rate of lactate production increasing with the rate of work.

2. Lactate is transported between muscle and blood, with the rate of transport depending on the concentrations in the two compartments

3. Lactate is cleared from the blood, with a rate that increases with the blood lactate concentration, up to a maximum value.

### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹) as $\lambda$, which we can model as linear in heart rate $h(t)$ at time $t$, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:

$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}}\,\tanh\\!\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

This system has 8 unknown parameters: V_m, V_b, P_m, P_b, C_{min}, \eta_{threshold}, \alpha, \beta. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the. uscle volume V_m.

  \rho = V_b / V_m

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:
  a_m = P_m / V_b
  a_b = P_b / V_b
  r_b = \eta_{threshold} / V_b

since:
  P_m / V_m = (P_m / V_b) (V_b / V_m) = \rho a_m 

we have the reparameterised model:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \rho a_m (C_m - C_b)
  dC_b/dt = a_m (C_m - C_b) - r_b tanh(a_b (C_b - C_{min}) / r_b)

 a_b > 0
 r_b > 0
 C_{min} > 0
 \alpha
 \beta > 0

where beta must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running.   \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)

input.new_string:
This system has 8 unknown parameters: $V_m$, $V_b$, $P_m$, $P_b$, $C_{\min}$, $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$, $\alpha$, $\beta$. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the muscle volume $V_m$.

We define $\rho$ as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:

$$\rho = \frac{V_b}{V_m}$$

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:

$$a_m = \frac{P_m}{V_b}, \qquad a_b = \frac{P_b}{V_b}, \qquad r_b = \frac{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}{V_b}$$

since

$$\frac{P_m}{V_m} = \frac{P_m}{V_b} \cdot \frac{V_b}{V_m} = \rho\, a_m$$

we have the reparameterised model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \rho\, a_m\,(C_m - C_b) \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= a_m\,(C_m - C_b) - r_b \, \tanh\left(\frac{a_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{r_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

with one fewer parameter:

$$\rho > 0, \quad a_m > 0, \quad a_b > 0, \quad r_b > 0, \quad C_{\min} > 0, \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \beta > 0$$

where $\beta$ must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and the others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

This is a simple test case of using agentic AI to perform amortised simulation-based Bayesian inference on a user-provided model and target dataset. This will use the agentic AI resources in https://github.com/idem-lab/bayesflow-sbi-agent-kit.

The main branch will contain the code and information prior to engaging agentic AI to support SBI. Worked agentic SBI analyses, with different versions of the above agent kit will be stored on different branchs.   

Note that the author is not an expert in exercise physiology, and the use case is a toy example, so the assumptions made in constructing this model should not be considered as remotely authoritative. Agentic AI is also being used to rapidly implement the initial code for this toy example and write documentation, so errors may well occur there too.

Below we define the model and data considered in this repo.

## Model

We define simple two-compartment model of lactate levels in muscle and blood, with a simple form of negative density dependence on lactate levels, under dynamic exercise load (external forcing). This model is inspired by the model of [Zouloumian & Freund (1981)](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428866), though that model focuses on lactate kinetics after the cessation of exercise, rather than under dynamic forcing of lactate.

### Assumptions:

1. Lactate is generated in working muscle, with the rate of lactate production increasing with the rate of work.

2. Lactate is transported between muscle and blood, with the rate of transport depending on the concentrations in the two compartments

3. Lactate is cleared from the blood, with a rate that increases with the blood lactate concentration, up to a maximum value.

### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

#### Lactate generation in muscle

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹) as $\lambda$, which we can model as linear in heart rate $h(t)$ at time $t$, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:

$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}} \, \tanh\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Reparameterisation

This system has 8 unknown parameters: V_m, V_b, P_m, P_b, C_{min}, \eta_{threshold}, \alpha, \beta. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the. uscle volume V_m.

We define rho as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:
  \rho = V_b / V_m

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:
  a_m = P_m / V_b
  a_b = P_b / V_b
  r_b = \eta_{threshold} / V_b

since:
  P_m / V_m = (P_m / V_b) (V_b / V_m) = \rho a_m 

we have the reparameterised model:

  dC_m/dt = \lambda - \rho a_m (C_m - C_b)
  dC_b/dt = a_m (C_m - C_b) - r_b tanh(a_b (C_b - C_{min}) / r_b)

with one fewer parameter:
 \rho > 0
 a_m > 0
 a_b > 0
 r_b > 0
 C_{min} > 0
 \alpha
 \beta > 0

where beta must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

### Sampling model

The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running. The protocol has participants warm up, stand stationary for 3 minutes, and then perform a continuous series of 3 minute efforts with increasing exercise intensity. Two relevant types of data are collected for each of these 3 minute periods (stationary and efforts)
Two types of data are available for each period, for each participant:
 - average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol.L^-1) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.

Since the amount of work is kept roughly constant within each period, we convert the average heart rate measuements into a piecewise constant continuous function on time: h(t). Given the reparameterised process equations and parameters listed above, we model a continuous-time function of blood lactate concentration as:

  \lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta h(t)
  C_b(t) = \int_0^t dC_b/dt dt

We define a Gaussian sampling model for each blood lactate concentration measurement B_i using this modelled function, where t(i) denotes the time corresponding to blood lactate observation i - the end of each 3 minute period - and an observation standard deviation parameter \sigma:

  B_i ~ N(C_b(t(i)), \sigma^2)

### Priors

We should be able to define an informative prior on sigma from literature on the measurement error of lactate tests. For all other parameters we will have to define reasonable priors by prior predictive checking. As a heuristic, blood lactate in a sports setting (ie. not in a medical emergency) has been observed as high as 32 mmol.L^-1 [(Osnes & Hermansen, 1972)](https://doi.org/10.1152/jappl.1972.32.1.59).

## Data

We will infer the model parameters for each participant in the cycling and running records of a large dataset of graded incremental exercise tests, which give these average heart rates and blood lactate measurements: [Bernard et al. (2024)](https://zenodo.org/records/10841412).

- average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol·L⁻¹) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.

# Lactate threshold modelling - simulation-based inference test case

This experimental repository defines and simulates from a mechanistic model of lactate accumulation under dynamic excercise loads. It will then build and validates an amortised Bayesian simulation-based inference engine, and applies it to empirical data with continuous heart rate monitoring and intermittent lactate testing, to infer a posterior over the lactate threshold and lactate threshold heart rate.

This is a simple test case of using agentic AI to perform amortised simulation-based Bayesian inference on a user-provided model and target dataset. This will use the agentic AI resources in https://github.com/idem-lab/bayesflow-sbi-agent-kit.

The main branch will contain the code and information prior to engaging agentic AI to support SBI. Worked agentic SBI analyses, with different versions of the above agent kit will be stored on different branchs.   

Note that the author is not an expert in exercise physiology, and the use case is a toy example, so the assumptions made in constructing this model should not be considered as remotely authoritative. Agentic AI is also being used to rapidly implement the initial code for this toy example and write documentation, so errors may well occur there too.

Below we define the model and data considered in this repo.

## Model

We define simple two-compartment model of lactate levels in muscle and blood, with a simple form of negative density dependence on lactate levels, under dynamic exercise load (external forcing). This model is inspired by the model of [Zouloumian & Freund (1981)](https://doi.org/10.1007/BF00428866), though that model focuses on lactate kinetics after the cessation of exercise, rather than under dynamic forcing of lactate.

### Assumptions:

1. Lactate is generated in working muscle, with the rate of lactate production increasing with the rate of work.

2. Lactate is transported between muscle and blood, with the rate of transport depending on the concentrations in the two compartments

3. Lactate is cleared from the blood, with a rate that increases with the blood lactate concentration, up to a maximum value.

### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:

$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}} \, \tanh\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Reparameterisation

This system has 8 unknown parameters: $V_m$, $V_b$, $P_m$, $P_b$, $C_{\min}$, $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$, $\alpha$, $\beta$. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the muscle volume $V_m$.

We define $\rho$ as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:

$$\rho = \frac{V_b}{V_m}$$

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:

$$a_m = \frac{P_m}{V_b}, \qquad a_b = \frac{P_b}{V_b}, \qquad r_b = \frac{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}{V_b}$$

since

$$\frac{P_m}{V_m} = \frac{P_m}{V_b} \cdot \frac{V_b}{V_m} = \rho\, a_m$$

we have the reparameterised model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \rho\, a_m\,(C_m - C_b) \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= a_m\,(C_m - C_b) - r_b \, \tanh\left(\frac{a_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{r_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

with one fewer parameter:

$$\rho > 0, \quad a_m > 0, \quad a_b > 0, \quad r_b > 0, \quad C_{\min} > 0, \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \beta > 0$$

We will infer the model parameters for each participant in the cycling and running records of a large dataset of graded incremental exercise tests, which give these average heart rates and blood lactate measurements: [Bernard et al. (2024)](https://zenodo.org/records/10841412).

For all other parameters we will have to define reasonable priors by prior predictive checking. As a heuristic, blood lactate in a sports setting (ie. not in a medical emergency) has been observed as high as 32 mmol.L^-1 [(Osnes & Hermansen, 1972)](https://doi.org/10.1152/jappl.1972.32.1.59).

## Data

We will infer the model parameters for each participant in the cycling and running records of a large dataset of graded incremental exercise tests, which give these average heart rates and blood lactate measurements: [Bernard et al. (2024)](https://zenodo.org/records/10841412).

25	### Process model:
26	
27	We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.
28	
29	#### Lactate generation in muscle
30	
31	We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹) as $\lambda$, which we can model as linear in heart rate $h(t)$ at time $t$, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:
32	
33	$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$
34	
35	where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.
36	
37	#### Lactate transfer
38	
39	We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:
40	
41	$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$
42	
43	#### Lactate clearance
44	
45	At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:
46	
47	$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}} \, \tanh\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$
48	
49	#### Kinetics
50	
51	We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:
52	
53	$$
54	\begin{aligned}
55	\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
56	\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
57	\end{aligned}
58	$$
59	
60	#### Reparameterisation
61	
62	This system has 8 unknown parameters: $V_m$, $V_b$, $P_m$, $P_b$, $C_{\min}$, $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$, $\alpha$, $\beta$. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the muscle volume $V_m$.
63	
64	We define $\rho$ as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:
65	
66	$$\rho = \frac{V_b}{V_m}$$
67	
68	We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:
69	
70	$$a_m = \frac{P_m}{V_b}, \qquad a_b = \frac{P_b}{V_b}, \qquad r_b = \frac{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}{V_b}$$
71	
72	since
73	
74	$$\frac{P_m}{V_m} = \frac{P_m}{V_b} \cdot \frac{V_b}{V_m} = \rho\, a_m$$
75	
76	we have the reparameterised model:
77	
78	$$
79	\begin{aligned}
80	\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \rho\, a_m\,(C_m - C_b) \\
81	\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= a_m\,(C_m - C_b) - r_b \, \tanh\left(\frac{a_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{r_b}\right)
82	\end{aligned}
83	$$
84	
85	with one fewer parameter:
86	
87	$$\rho > 0, \quad a_m > 0, \quad a_b > 0, \quad r_b > 0, \quad C_{\min} > 0, \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \beta > 0$$
88	
89	where $\beta$ must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and the others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).
90	
91	### Sampling model
92	
93	The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running. The protocol has participants warm up, stand stationary for 3 minutes, and then perform a continuous series of 3 minute efforts with increasing exercise intensity. Two relevant types of data are collected for each of these 3 minute periods (stationary and efforts)
94	Two types of data are available for each period, for each participant:
95	 - average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
96	 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol·L⁻¹) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.
97	
98	Since the amount of work is kept roughly constant within each period, we convert the average heart rate measuements into a piecewise constant continuous function on time: $h(t)$. Given the reparameterised process equations and parameters listed above, we model a continuous-time function of blood lactate concentration as:
99	
100	$$
101	\begin{aligned}
102	\lambda(t) &= \alpha + \beta\, h(t) \\
103	C_b(t) &= C_b(0) + \int_0^t \frac{dC_b}{ds}\, ds
104	\end{aligned}
105	$$
106	
107	We define a Gaussian sampling model for each blood lactate concentration measurement $B_i$ using this modelled function, where $t(i)$ denotes the time corresponding to blood lactate observation $i$ — the end of each 3 minute period — and an observation standard deviation parameter $\sigma$:
108	
109	$$B_i \sim \mathcal{N}\left(C_b(t(i)),\, \sigma^2\right)$$

file.content:
### Process model:

We model kinetics of the concentration of lactate (mmol·L⁻¹) in the muscle ($C_m$) and blood ($C_b$) during exercise via three processes: lactate genertion in muscle, lactate transfer from muscle to blood, and lactate clearing from blood.

#### Lactate generation in muscle

We parameterise the rate of lactate concentration accumulation in the muscle compartment (mmol·L⁻¹·min⁻¹) as $\lambda$, which we can model as linear in heart rate $h(t)$ at time $t$, as a proxy for the amount of work being done by the muscle:

$$\lambda(t) = \alpha + \beta\, h(t)$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are unconstrained (real-valued) free scalar parameters. This term governs external forcing of lactate in the system.

#### Lactate transfer

We model the rate of transfer of lactate mass (mmol·min⁻¹, note: not concentration) away from muscle and into blood as proportional to the chemical gradient (difference in concentration between muscle and blood) and a unitless 'permeability parameter' $P_m$:

$$\eta_m = P_m\,(C_m - C_b)$$

#### Lactate clearance

At 'low' blood lactate concentrations (below some threshold transport rate $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$), lactate is modelled as being cleared from the blood with some permeability $P_b$, proportionally to its concentration above a minimum value $C_{\min}$; analagous to a permeable membrane with a fixed low concentration in on the other side. However at high blood lactate concentrations this membrane saturates, and the gradient can no longer increase. We model this with a tanh function, and the threshold transport rate:

$$\eta_b = \eta_{\text{threshold}} \, \tanh\left(\frac{P_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}\right)$$

#### Kinetics

We model the lactate kinetics with two differential equations on the concentrations of lactate in muscle and in blood, by adjusting these absolute flow rates to the concentrations of the respective compartments:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \frac{\eta_m}{V_m} \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= \frac{\eta_m}{V_b} - \frac{\eta_b}{V_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Reparameterisation

This system has 8 unknown parameters: $V_m$, $V_b$, $P_m$, $P_b$, $C_{\min}$, $\eta_{\text{threshold}}$, $\alpha$, $\beta$. While some can be jointly inferred from heart rate and blood lactate data, others are not identified. In particular, the total volumes of muscle and blood cannot be identified from only concentration data. We therefore reparameterise this relative to the muscle volume $V_m$.

We define $\rho$ as the ratio of blood to muscle volumes:

$$\rho = \frac{V_b}{V_m}$$

We scale the permeability and threshold transfer parameters by the blood volume:

$$a_m = \frac{P_m}{V_b}, \qquad a_b = \frac{P_b}{V_b}, \qquad r_b = \frac{\eta_{\text{threshold}}}{V_b}$$

since

$$\frac{P_m}{V_m} = \frac{P_m}{V_b} \cdot \frac{V_b}{V_m} = \rho\, a_m$$

we have the reparameterised model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_m}{dt} &= \lambda - \rho\, a_m\,(C_m - C_b) \\
\frac{dC_b}{dt} &= a_m\,(C_m - C_b) - r_b \, \tanh\left(\frac{a_b\,(C_b - C_{\min})}{r_b}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

with one fewer parameter:

$$\rho > 0, \quad a_m > 0, \quad a_b > 0, \quad r_b > 0, \quad C_{\min} > 0, \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{R}, \quad \beta > 0$$

where $\beta$ must be positive because the increase in lactate concentration is assumed to increase with heart rate, and the others are positive by construction (ratios and rates).

### Sampling model

The data available to infer these parameters are from graded incremental exercise tests for various participants in cycling and running. The protocol has participants warm up, stand stationary for 3 minutes, and then perform a continuous series of 3 minute efforts with increasing exercise intensity. Two relevant types of data are collected for each of these 3 minute periods (stationary and efforts)
Two types of data are available for each period, for each participant:
 - average heart rate over the period, in beats per minute (bpm)
 - the measured concentration of lactate in the blood in millimoles per litre (mmol·L⁻¹) in a small blood sample taken at the end of the 3 minute period.

Since the amount of work is kept roughly constant within each period, we convert the average heart rate measuements into a piecewise constant continuous function on time: $h(t)$. Given the reparameterised process equations and parameters listed above, we model a continuous-time function of blood lactate concentration as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda(t) &= \alpha + \beta\, h(t) \\
C_b(t) &= C_b(0) + \int_0^t \frac{dC_b}{ds}\, ds
\end{aligned}
$$

We define a Gaussian sampling model for each blood lactate concentration measurement $B_i$ using this modelled function, where $t(i)$ denotes the time corresponding to blood lactate observation $i$ — the end of each 3 minute period — and an observation standard deviation parameter $\sigma$:

$$B_i \sim \mathcal{N}\left(C_b(t(i)),\, \sigma^2\right)$$

input.old_string:
# NOTE 1  (sign convention).
#   The README's final *boxed* reparameterised equations write the muscle-blood
#   gradient as (C_b - C_m):
#       dC_m/dt = lambda - rho*a_m*(C_b - C_m)
#       dC_b/dt = a_m*(C_b - C_m) - ...
#   but the derivation immediately above them defines
#       eta_m   = P_m*(C_m - C_b),   dC_m/dt = lambda - eta_m/V_m,
#       dC_b/dt = eta_m/V_b - eta_b/V_b,
#   which gives the gradient as (C_m - C_b). The two disagree by a sign in both
#   equations. The (C_b - C_m) form is physically unstable (muscle lactate has
#   no route out and diverges), so this file implements the derivation-
#   consistent (C_m - C_b) form. Set `gradient_sign = -1` to reproduce the
#   boxed form exactly if that is what you intended.
#
# NOTE 2  (C_min).
#   The README says the reparameterised model has "two fewer" than 8 parameters
#   and lists six (rho, a_m, a_b, r_b, alpha, beta), but C_min still appears in
#   the clearance term and is needed to integrate. It is therefore kept here as
#   an explicit argument (default 0.8 mmol/L, a typical resting value) so you
#   can either fix it or treat it as free.
input.new_string:
# NOTE 1  (sign convention).
#   The README uses the muscle-blood gradient (C_m - C_b): muscle produces
#   lactate and it flows to blood down the concentration gradient. This file
#   implements that derivation-consistent, physically stable form, which is the
#   default (gradient_sign = +1). An earlier draft of the README had the sign
#   flipped in the boxed equations (C_b - C_m), which is physically unstable
#   (muscle lactate has no route out and diverges); `gradient_sign = -1`
#   reproduces that flipped form should you ever need to compare against it.
#
# NOTE 2  (C_min).
#   The README lists C_min > 0 as one of the seven reparameterised parameters
#   (rho, a_m, a_b, r_b, C_min, alpha, beta). It is kept here as an explicit
#   argument (default 0.8 mmol/L, a typical resting value) so you can either fix
#   it or treat it as free.

content.text:
Everything checks out. Here's the summary.

## Your corrections — both confirmed fixed ✅

1. **Sign flip:** the reparameterised ODEs (README lines 80–81) now read `(C_m - C_b)` in both equations, matching the `η_m = P_m(C_m − C_b)` derivation above. The physically-stable form.
2. **`C_min`:** the parameter list now says "with one fewer parameter" and includes `C_{min} > 0` — 7 parameters (8 → 7), internally consistent, and `C_min` is no longer an unlisted term in the clearance equation.

I also fixed two incidental typos I hit while reformatting: `to the. uscle volume` → `to the muscle volume`, and the missing backslash in `eta_b` in the kinetics equation.

## Equation reformatting

Every equation in the **Process model → Sampling model** sections is now GitHub-native LaTeX math — `$…$` inline for symbols in prose and `$$…$$` blocks (with `\begin{aligned}` for the paired ODEs) for displays. GitHub renders these via MathJax. Also converted the unit strings (`mmol.L^-1` → `mmol·L⁻¹` etc.) to Unicode superscripts so they read cleanly without a code font.

One small judgment call to flag: I rewrote your `C_b(t) = ∫₀ᵗ dC_b/dt dt` as

$$C_b(t) = C_b(0) + \int_0^t \frac{dC_b}{ds}\, ds$$

i.e. added the initial condition and a distinct dummy variable `s`, since the integrand and limit shouldn't share the symbol `t`. If you'd rather keep it closer to your original, say so and I'll revert to a literal reformat.

## Code kept in sync

Since the README no longer contains those discrepancies, I updated the two `NOTE` blocks in `R/simulate.R` to describe the corrected model (the `gradient_sign = -1` escape hatch stays, now framed as "reproduce the earlier flipped draft for comparison"), and tightened `lactate_params()` to enforce `C_min > 0` to match the README. Re-tested: default simulation runs, `C_min = 0` is now rejected, and the flipped form still diverges as expected.

I have **not** rendered the README to verify the math visually (I can't run GitHub's MathJax locally) — the syntax is standard and correct, but you may want a quick look at the rendered page. Want me to also add anchor-friendly spacing or a rendered-equations preview via an artifact?

message.content: I've rendered the readme, it looks fine. Good catch on the integrand symbol. Please remove the gradient sign parameter and the note about C_min.

content.content:
1	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
2	# simulate.R
3	#
4	# Forward simulator for the two-compartment (muscle / blood) lactate model
5	# described in the repository README, under a dynamic heart-rate forcing.
6	#
7	# Reparameterised process model (README "Reparameterisation" section):
8	#
9	#   lambda(t) = alpha + beta * h(t)                         # muscle production
10	#   dC_m/dt   = lambda      - rho * a_m * (C_m - C_b)       # muscle balance
11	#   dC_b/dt   = a_m * (C_m - C_b) - r_b * tanh(a_b*(C_b - C_min)/r_b)  # blood
12	#
13	# and the Gaussian sampling model for the end-of-period blood-lactate samples:
14	#
15	#   B_i ~ Normal( C_b(t(i)), sigma^2 )
16	#
17	# with parameters
18	#   rho   > 0   ratio of blood to muscle volume  (V_b / V_m)
19	#   a_m   > 0   muscle<->blood permeability / V_b
20	#   a_b   > 0   blood clearance permeability / V_b
21	#   r_b   > 0   saturating (threshold) blood clearance rate / V_b
22	#   alpha       intercept of muscle production (real-valued)
23	#   beta  > 0   slope of muscle production in heart rate
24	#   C_min       minimum blood lactate (clearance floor)      [see NOTE 2]
25	#   sigma > 0   blood-lactate measurement standard deviation
26	#
27	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
28	# NOTE 1  (sign convention).
29	#   The README uses the muscle-blood gradient (C_m - C_b): muscle produces
30	#   lactate and it flows to blood down the concentration gradient. This file
31	#   implements that derivation-consistent, physically stable form, which is the
32	#   default (gradient_sign = +1). An earlier draft of the README had the sign
33	#   flipped in the boxed equations (C_b - C_m), which is physically unstable
34	#   (muscle lactate has no route out and diverges); `gradient_sign = -1`
35	#   reproduces that flipped form should you ever need to compare against it.
36	#
37	# NOTE 2  (C_min).
38	#   The README lists C_min > 0 as one of the seven reparameterised parameters
39	#   (rho, a_m, a_b, r_b, C_min, alpha, beta). It is kept here as an explicit
40	#   argument (default 0.8 mmol/L, a typical resting value) so you can either fix
41	#   it or treat it as free.
42	#
43	# Requires: deSolve  (install.packages("deSolve"))
44	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
45	
46	library(deSolve)
47	
48	
49	#' Construct and validate a parameter set for the lactate model.
50	#'
51	#' @return A named list of the eight model parameters, with defaults chosen to
52	#'   produce a plausible incremental-test lactate curve. All positivity
53	#'   constraints from the README are enforced.
54	lactate_params <- function(rho   = 3.0,
55	                           a_m   = 0.30,
56	                           a_b   = 0.60,
57	                           r_b   = 2.0,
58	                           alpha = -0.60,
59	                           beta  = 0.010,
60	                           C_min = 0.8,
61	                           sigma = 0.3) {
62	  p <- list(rho = rho, a_m = a_m, a_b = a_b, r_b = r_b,
63	            alpha = alpha, beta = beta, C_min = C_min, sigma = sigma)
64	  pos <- c("rho", "a_m", "a_b", "r_b", "C_min", "beta", "sigma")
65	  for (nm in pos) {
66	    if (!is.finite(p[[nm]]) || p[[nm]] <= 0)
67	      stop(sprintf("Parameter '%s' must be finite and > 0 (got %s).",
68	                   nm, format(p[[nm]])))
69	  }
70	  if (!is.finite(p$alpha)) stop("Parameter 'alpha' must be finite.")
71	  p
72	}
73	
74	
75	#' Build a piecewise-constant heart-rate forcing function h(t).
76	#'
77	#' Heart rate `hr[i]` is the average over period i, so h(t) is constant on the
78	#' interval ((i-1)*period, i*period]. `t` is in minutes.
79	#'
80	#' @param hr             Numeric vector of per-period average heart rates (bpm).
81	#' @param period_minutes Length of each period in minutes (default 3).
82	#' @return A function h(t) (vectorised over t).
83	make_hr_forcing <- function(hr, period_minutes = 3) {
84	  if (any(!is.finite(hr)))
85	    stop("Heart-rate series contains non-finite values; ",
86	         "clean or interpolate before simulating.")
87	  n     <- length(hr)
88	  knots <- (0:n) * period_minutes          # 0, period, 2*period, ..., n*period
89	  vals  <- c(hr, hr[n])                     # value on [knot_j, knot_{j+1})
90	  approxfun(knots, vals, method = "constant", rule = 2, f = 0)
91	}
92	
93	
94	#' Right-hand side of the two-compartment ODE system (for deSolve::ode).
95	#'
96	#' @param t     Time (minutes).
97	#' @param y     Named state c(C_m = ..., C_b = ...).
98	#' @param parms List with model parameters, an `hr_fun` forcing function, and a
99	#'   `gradient_sign` (see NOTE 1).
100	lactate_rhs <- function(t, y, parms) {
101	  C_m <- y[["C_m"]]
102	  C_b <- y[["C_b"]]
103	
104	  h      <- parms$hr_fun(t)
105	  lambda <- parms$alpha + parms$beta * h                 # muscle production
106	
107	  # gradient_sign = +1 -> derivation-consistent (C_m - C_b) form (default)
108	  # gradient_sign = -1 -> README boxed (C_b - C_m) form
109	  grad <- parms$gradient_sign * (C_m - C_b)
110	
111	  transfer  <- parms$a_m * grad                          # muscle -> blood flux
112	  clearance <- parms$r_b * tanh(parms$a_b * (C_b - parms$C_min) / parms$r_b)
113	
114	  dC_m <- lambda - parms$rho * transfer
115	  dC_b <- transfer - clearance
116	
117	  list(c(C_m = dC_m, C_b = dC_b), lambda = lambda)
118	}
119	
120	
121	#' Simulate the latent lactate trajectories C_m(t) and C_b(t).
122	#'
123	#' Integrates the ODE system under the heart-rate forcing implied by `hr`, and
124	#' returns the modelled concentrations at the requested times.
125	#'
126	#' @param params         A list from `lactate_params()`.
127	#' @param hr             Per-period average heart rates (bpm).
128	#' @param period_minutes Period length, minutes (default 3).
129	#' @param obs_times      Times (minutes) at which to report the trajectory.
130	#'   Default: end of each period, i.e. period_minutes * seq_along(hr) — the
131	#'   times t(i) at which blood lactate is sampled.
132	#' @param y0             Initial state c(C_m, C_b). Default: both at C_min
133	#'   (resting baseline).
134	#' @param gradient_sign  +1 (default, derivation-consistent) or -1 (README
135	#'   boxed form). See NOTE 1.
136	#' @param ...            Passed to deSolve::ode (e.g. method, rtol, atol).
137	#' @return A data.frame with columns `time_min`, `C_m`, `C_b`, `lambda`.
138	simulate_lactate <- function(params,
139	                             hr,
140	                             period_minutes = 3,
141	                             obs_times      = NULL,
142	                             y0             = NULL,
143	                             gradient_sign  = 1,
144	                             ...) {
145	  if (is.null(obs_times))
146	    obs_times <- period_minutes * seq_along(hr)
147	  if (is.null(y0))
148	    y0 <- c(C_m = params$C_min, C_b = params$C_min)
149	
150	  parms <- c(params,
151	             list(hr_fun        = make_hr_forcing(hr, period_minutes),
152	                  gradient_sign = gradient_sign))
153	
154	  # Integrate from 0 through all requested times. deSolve needs t = 0 in the
155	  # time grid for the initial condition; we prepend it and drop it after.
156	  times <- sort(unique(c(0, obs_times)))
157	  sol   <- deSolve::ode(y = y0, times = times, func = lactate_rhs,
158	                        parms = parms, ...)
159	  sol   <- as.data.frame(sol)
160	
161	  out <- sol[sol$time %in% obs_times, , drop = FALSE]
162	  data.frame(time_min = out$time,
163	             C_m      = out$C_m,
164	             C_b      = out$C_b,
165	             lambda   = out$lambda,
166	             row.names = NULL)
167	}
168	
169	
170	#' Simulate observed blood-lactate samples B_i from the model.
171	#'
172	#' Runs the forward model and adds Gaussian measurement noise with standard
173	#' deviation `params$sigma`, per the README sampling model
174	#' B_i ~ Normal(C_b(t(i)), sigma^2).
175	#'
176	#' @inheritParams simulate_lactate
177	#' @return A data.frame with columns `period`, `time_min`, `hr`, `C_b`
178	#'   (latent), and `bla` (noisy simulated observation).
179	simulate_lactate_obs <- function(params,
180	                                 hr,
181	                                 period_minutes = 3,
182	                                 obs_times      = NULL,
183	                                 y0             = NULL,
184	                                 gradient_sign  = 1,
185	                                 ...) {
186	  if (is.null(obs_times))
187	    obs_times <- period_minutes * seq_along(hr)
188	
189	  traj <- simulate_lactate(params, hr, period_minutes = period_minutes,
190	                           obs_times = obs_times, y0 = y0,
191	                           gradient_sign = gradient_sign, ...)
192	
193	  noise <- rnorm(nrow(traj), mean = 0, sd = params$sigma)
194	  data.frame(period   = seq_len(nrow(traj)),
195	             time_min = traj$time_min,
196	             hr       = hr[seq_len(nrow(traj))],
197	             C_b      = traj$C_b,
198	             bla      = traj$C_b + noise,
199	             row.names = NULL)
200	}
201	
202	
203	#' Draw a parameter set from a (placeholder) prior.
204	#'
205	#' The README states that priors other than sigma are to be pinned down by
206	#' prior-predictive checking; these are weakly-informative placeholders that
207	#' respect the positivity/support constraints (log-normal for the positive
208	#' parameters, normal for the real-valued alpha). Tune the hyperparameters as
209	#' the prior-predictive work proceeds.
210	#'
211	#' @return A list in the same shape as `lactate_params()`.
212	sample_prior <- function() {
213	  ln <- function(meanlog, sdlog) exp(rnorm(1, meanlog, sdlog))
214	  lactate_params(
215	    rho   = ln(log(3.0),  0.4),
216	    a_m   = ln(log(0.30), 0.5),
217	    a_b   = ln(log(0.60), 0.5),
218	    r_b   = ln(log(2.0),  0.5),
219	    alpha = rnorm(1, -0.6, 0.4),
220	    beta  = ln(log(0.010), 0.4),
221	    C_min = ln(log(0.8),  0.25),
222	    sigma = ln(log(0.3),  0.3)      # informative-ish; refine from lit. (README)
223	  )
224	}
225	
226	
227	#' Simulate a full synthetic test given a heart-rate protocol.
228	#'
229	#' Convenience wrapper: draw parameters (or use supplied ones) and produce a
230	#' synthetic set of blood-lactate observations for a given HR series. Useful for
231	#' generating training/validation data or for prior-predictive checks.
232	#'
233	#' @param hr             Per-period average heart rates (bpm).
234	#' @param params         Parameter list; if NULL, drawn from `sample_prior()`.
235	#' @param period_minutes Period length, minutes (default 3).
236	#' @param gradient_sign  See NOTE 1.
237	#' @return A list with `params` (the parameters used) and `data` (the
238	#'   simulated per-period data.frame from `simulate_lactate_obs`).
239	simulate_test <- function(hr,
240	                          params         = NULL,
241	                          period_minutes = 3,
242	                          gradient_sign  = 1) {
243	  if (is.null(params)) params <- sample_prior()
244	  dat <- simulate_lactate_obs(params, hr, period_minutes = period_minutes,
245	                              gradient_sign = gradient_sign)
246	  list(params = params, data = dat)
247	}
248	
249	
250	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
251	# Example (not run on source):
252	#
253	#   source("R/simulate.R")
254	#
255	#   # A heart-rate protocol: 3 min rest then rising efforts (bpm).
256	#   hr <- c(74, 122, 128, 140, 150, 158, 164, 169, 179, 184, 190)
257	#
258	#   # Forward-simulate the latent lactate curve with default parameters:
259	#   p    <- lactate_params()
260	#   traj <- simulate_lactate(p, hr)          # time_min, C_m, C_b, lambda
261	#
262	#   # Simulate noisy blood-lactate observations B_i:
263	#   obs  <- simulate_lactate_obs(p, hr)      # ... plus a 'bla' column
264	#
265	#   # Draw parameters from the prior and simulate a whole synthetic test:
266	#   sim  <- simulate_test(hr)

file.content:
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# simulate.R
#
# Forward simulator for the two-compartment (muscle / blood) lactate model
# described in the repository README, under a dynamic heart-rate forcing.
#
# Reparameterised process model (README "Reparameterisation" section):
#
#   lambda(t) = alpha + beta * h(t)                         # muscle production
#   dC_m/dt   = lambda      - rho * a_m * (C_m - C_b)       # muscle balance
#   dC_b/dt   = a_m * (C_m - C_b) - r_b * tanh(a_b*(C_b - C_min)/r_b)  # blood
#
# and the Gaussian sampling model for the end-of-period blood-lactate samples:
#
#   B_i ~ Normal( C_b(t(i)), sigma^2 )
#
# with parameters
#   rho   > 0   ratio of blood to muscle volume  (V_b / V_m)
#   a_m   > 0   muscle<->blood permeability / V_b
#   a_b   > 0   blood clearance permeability / V_b
#   r_b   > 0   saturating (threshold) blood clearance rate / V_b
#   alpha       intercept of muscle production (real-valued)
#   beta  > 0   slope of muscle production in heart rate
#   C_min       minimum blood lactate (clearance floor)      [see NOTE 2]
#   sigma > 0   blood-lactate measurement standard deviation
#
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# NOTE 1  (sign convention).
#   The README uses the muscle-blood gradient (C_m - C_b): muscle produces
#   lactate and it flows to blood down the concentration gradient. This file
#   implements that derivation-consistent, physically stable form, which is the
#   default (gradient_sign = +1). An earlier draft of the README had the sign
#   flipped in the boxed equations (C_b - C_m), which is physically unstable
#   (muscle lactate has no route out and diverges); `gradient_sign = -1`
#   reproduces that flipped form should you ever need to compare against it.
#
# NOTE 2  (C_min).
#   The README lists C_min > 0 as one of the seven reparameterised parameters
#   (rho, a_m, a_b, r_b, C_min, alpha, beta). It is kept here as an explicit
#   argument (default 0.8 mmol/L, a typical resting value) so you can either fix
#   it or treat it as free.
#
# Requires: deSolve  (install.packages("deSolve"))
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

library(deSolve)


#' Construct and validate a parameter set for the lactate model.
#'
#' @return A named list of the eight model parameters, with defaults chosen to
#'   produce a plausible incremental-test lactate curve. All positivity
#'   constraints from the README are enforced.
lactate_params <- function(rho   = 3.0,
                           a_m   = 0.30,
                           a_b   = 0.60,
                           r_b   = 2.0,
                           alpha = -0.60,
                           beta  = 0.010,
                           C_min = 0.8,
                           sigma = 0.3) {
  p <- list(rho = rho, a_m = a_m, a_b = a_b, r_b = r_b,
            alpha = alpha, beta = beta, C_min = C_min, sigma = sigma)
  pos <- c("rho", "a_m", "a_b", "r_b", "C_min", "beta", "sigma")
  for (nm in pos) {
    if (!is.finite(p[[nm]]) || p[[nm]] <= 0)
      stop(sprintf("Parameter '%s' must be finite and > 0 (got %s).",
                   nm, format(p[[nm]])))
  }
  if (!is.finite(p$alpha)) stop("Parameter 'alpha' must be finite.")
  p
}


#' Build a piecewise-constant heart-rate forcing function h(t).
#'
#' Heart rate `hr[i]` is the average over period i, so h(t) is constant on the
#' interval ((i-1)*period, i*period]. `t` is in minutes.
#'
#' @param hr             Numeric vector of per-period average heart rates (bpm).
#' @param period_minutes Length of each period in minutes (default 3).
#' @return A function h(t) (vectorised over t).
make_hr_forcing <- function(hr, period_minutes = 3) {
  if (any(!is.finite(hr)))
    stop("Heart-rate series contains non-finite values; ",
         "clean or interpolate before simulating.")
  n     <- length(hr)
  knots <- (0:n) * period_minutes          # 0, period, 2*period, ..., n*period
  vals  <- c(hr, hr[n])                     # value on [knot_j, knot_{j+1})
  approxfun(knots, vals, method = "constant", rule = 2, f = 0)
}


#' Right-hand side of the two-compartment ODE system (for deSolve::ode).
#'
#' @param t     Time (minutes).
#' @param y     Named state c(C_m = ..., C_b = ...).
#' @param parms List with model parameters, an `hr_fun` forcing function, and a
#'   `gradient_sign` (see NOTE 1).
lactate_rhs <- function(t, y, parms) {
  C_m <- y[["C_m"]]
  C_b <- y[["C_b"]]

  h      <- parms$hr_fun(t)
  lambda <- parms$alpha + parms$beta * h                 # muscle production

  # gradient_sign = +1 -> derivation-consistent (C_m - C_b) form (default)
  # gradient_sign = -1 -> README boxed (C_b - C_m) form
  grad <- parms$gradient_sign * (C_m - C_b)

  transfer  <- parms$a_m * grad                          # muscle -> blood flux
  clearance <- parms$r_b * tanh(parms$a_b * (C_b - parms$C_min) / parms$r_b)

  dC_m <- lambda - parms$rho * transfer
  dC_b <- transfer - clearance

  list(c(C_m = dC_m, C_b = dC_b), lambda = lambda)
}


#' Simulate the latent lactate trajectories C_m(t) and C_b(t).
#'
#' Integrates the ODE system under the heart-rate forcing implied by `hr`, and
#' returns the modelled concentrations at the requested times.
#'
#' @param params         A list from `lactate_params()`.
#' @param hr             Per-period average heart rates (bpm).
#' @param period_minutes Period length, minutes (default 3).
#' @param obs_times      Times (minutes) at which to report the trajectory.
#'   Default: end of each period, i.e. period_minutes * seq_along(hr) — the
#'   times t(i) at which blood lactate is sampled.
#' @param y0             Initial state c(C_m, C_b). Default: both at C_min
#'   (resting baseline).
#' @param gradient_sign  +1 (default, derivation-consistent) or -1 (README
#'   boxed form). See NOTE 1.
#' @param ...            Passed to deSolve::ode (e.g. method, rtol, atol).
#' @return A data.frame with columns `time_min`, `C_m`, `C_b`, `lambda`.
simulate_lactate <- function(params,
                             hr,
                             period_minutes = 3,
                             obs_times      = NULL,
                             y0             = NULL,
                             gradient_sign  = 1,
                             ...) {
  if (is.null(obs_times))
    obs_times <- period_minutes * seq_along(hr)
  if (is.null(y0))
    y0 <- c(C_m = params$C_min, C_b = params$C_min)

  parms <- c(params,
             list(hr_fun        = make_hr_forcing(hr, period_minutes),
                  gradient_sign = gradient_sign))

  # Integrate from 0 through all requested times. deSolve needs t = 0 in the
  # time grid for the initial condition; we prepend it and drop it after.
  times <- sort(unique(c(0, obs_times)))
  sol   <- deSolve::ode(y = y0, times = times, func = lactate_rhs,
                        parms = parms, ...)
  sol   <- as.data.frame(sol)

  out <- sol[sol$time %in% obs_times, , drop = FALSE]
  data.frame(time_min = out$time,
             C_m      = out$C_m,
             C_b      = out$C_b,
             lambda   = out$lambda,
             row.names = NULL)
}


#' Simulate observed blood-lactate samples B_i from the model.
#'
#' Runs the forward model and adds Gaussian measurement noise with standard
#' deviation `params$sigma`, per the README sampling model
#' B_i ~ Normal(C_b(t(i)), sigma^2).
#'
#' @inheritParams simulate_lactate
#' @return A data.frame with columns `period`, `time_min`, `hr`, `C_b`
#'   (latent), and `bla` (noisy simulated observation).
simulate_lactate_obs <- function(params,
                                 hr,
                                 period_minutes = 3,
                                 obs_times      = NULL,
                                 y0             = NULL,
                                 gradient_sign  = 1,
                                 ...) {
  if (is.null(obs_times))
    obs_times <- period_minutes * seq_along(hr)

  traj <- simulate_lactate(params, hr, period_minutes = period_minutes,
                           obs_times = obs_times, y0 = y0,
                           gradient_sign = gradient_sign, ...)

  noise <- rnorm(nrow(traj), mean = 0, sd = params$sigma)
  data.frame(period   = seq_len(nrow(traj)),
             time_min = traj$time_min,
             hr       = hr[seq_len(nrow(traj))],
             C_b      = traj$C_b,
             bla      = traj$C_b + noise,
             row.names = NULL)
}


#' Draw a parameter set from a (placeholder) prior.
#'
#' The README states that priors other than sigma are to be pinned down by
#' prior-predictive checking; these are weakly-informative placeholders that
#' respect the positivity/support constraints (log-normal for the positive
#' parameters, normal for the real-valued alpha). Tune the hyperparameters as
#' the prior-predictive work proceeds.
#'
#' @return A list in the same shape as `lactate_params()`.
sample_prior <- function() {
  ln <- function(meanlog, sdlog) exp(rnorm(1, meanlog, sdlog))
  lactate_params(
    rho   = ln(log(3.0),  0.4),
    a_m   = ln(log(0.30), 0.5),
    a_b   = ln(log(0.60), 0.5),
    r_b   = ln(log(2.0),  0.5),
    alpha = rnorm(1, -0.6, 0.4),
    beta  = ln(log(0.010), 0.4),
    C_min = ln(log(0.8),  0.25),
    sigma = ln(log(0.3),  0.3)      # informative-ish; refine from lit. (README)
  )
}


#' Simulate a full synthetic test given a heart-rate protocol.
#'
#' Convenience wrapper: draw parameters (or use supplied ones) and produce a
#' synthetic set of blood-lactate observations for a given HR series. Useful for
#' generating training/validation data or for prior-predictive checks.
#'
#' @param hr             Per-period average heart rates (bpm).
#' @param params         Parameter list; if NULL, drawn from `sample_prior()`.
#' @param period_minutes Period length, minutes (default 3).
#' @param gradient_sign  See NOTE 1.
#' @return A list with `params` (the parameters used) and `data` (the
#'   simulated per-period data.frame from `simulate_lactate_obs`).
simulate_test <- function(hr,
                          params         = NULL,
                          period_minutes = 3,
                          gradient_sign  = 1) {
  if (is.null(params)) params <- sample_prior()
  dat <- simulate_lactate_obs(params, hr, period_minutes = period_minutes,
                              gradient_sign = gradient_sign)
  list(params = params, data = dat)
}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Example (not run on source):
#
#   source("R/simulate.R")
#
#   # A heart-rate protocol: 3 min rest then rising efforts (bpm).
#   hr <- c(74, 122, 128, 140, 150, 158, 164, 169, 179, 184, 190)
#
#   # Forward-simulate the latent lactate curve with default parameters:
#   p    <- lactate_params()
#   traj <- simulate_lactate(p, hr)          # time_min, C_m, C_b, lambda
#
#   # Simulate noisy blood-lactate observations B_i:
#   obs  <- simulate_lactate_obs(p, hr)      # ... plus a 'bla' column
#
#   # Draw parameters from the prior and simulate a whole synthetic test:
#   sim  <- simulate_test(hr)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

#   The README uses the muscle-blood gradient (C_m - C_b): muscle produces
